title: Inference parallelism: pipeline vs tensor

sources.notes: Answer key item in x_search synthesis tied to pipeline parallelism vs per-user token latency
Parent thread: interconnect latency as inference binding constraint

game_hooks.tone: Pressure-test the mechanism, not the buzzwords.

questions.stem: For per-user token latency on a large autoregressive model when links are high-latency, which sharding strategy usually reduces per-chip memory bandwidth pressure more directly?
choices: Tensor parallelism (layers split across chips with frequent all-reduces)
Pipeline parallelism (layers staged; activations are small payloads between stages)
Data parallelism only
Increasing batch size on a single chip
questions.explanation: Pipeline parallelism shards layers so each chip holds a slice of the stack; between stages the payload is small (e.g. activations on the order of KB), which helps when the bottleneck is memory wait and link latency rather than raw FLOPs.
questions.source_anchor: BadB01E thread synthesis: pipeline vs tensor for per-user token speed under high-latency links

questions.stem: Why does autoregressive decode often show low effective FLOP utilization on GPUs built for training-scale collectives?
choices: Activation functions dominate compute
The chip stalls waiting on memory while most FLOPs sit idle
KV cache is always too large for SRAM
All-reduce is free on inference
questions.explanation: Decode is memory-latency sensitive; oversized FLOP arrays relative to the memory pipe leave silicon idle while waiting on weights/KV traffic.
questions.source_anchor: BadB01E thread: low MFU on decode despite large dies

questions.stem: In the thread's framing, what is argued to be an even sharper binding constraint than memory bandwidth alone for scaling inference?
choices: Peak FP8 FLOPs
Interconnect latency (and how it shapes the whole system)
CUDA version
Cooling capacity
questions.explanation: The thesis works backward from link latency: it dictates memory choices, disaggregation, and how much compute to provision per byte delivered.

questions.stem: A proposed 'balance the pipeline' chip provisions ~280 TFLOPS FP4 instead of ~1250 TFLOPS. What reasoning supports that?
choices: Training needs fewer FLOPs than inference
Match FLOPs to the memory bandwidth pipe (~250 FLOPs per delivered byte at stated batch/precision), not maximal FLOPs
LPDDR cannot do FP4
SerDes ports replace the MAC array
questions.explanation: Over-provisioning FLOPs relative to the memory/interconnect pipe wastes area and power; the design sizes compute to what the pipe can feed.

questions.stem: Inside the proposed low-latency pod, what topology is used for the latency-critical domain?
choices: Centralized NVLink switch only
Full mesh across eight chips (multiple ports per chip)
Pure dragonfly at chip scale
3D torus inside each die
questions.explanation: An 8-chip full mesh minimizes hop count for the domain where tensor-style coordination must stay fast.

questions.stem: NO-HINT: You operate inference for 70B-class models. Users complain about time-to-first-token after a long context fill. Your links have ~microsecond-scale switch hops but HBM pools are small per chip. Without naming any blog post, what design move most directly attacks capacity + hop count together?
choices: Double FP4 FLOPs on each GPU regardless of memory
Disaggregate with a low-hop pod and favor memory technologies that increase GB per TB/s of effective bandwidth
Disable KV cache
Only use data parallelism
questions.explanation: Long context blows KV footprint; more local capacity per bandwidth dollar plus low-hop domains reduces round-trips that compound latency.
questions.source_anchor: Thread theme: capacity per TB/s and pod latency

critic_notes: Pack derived from x_search synthesis of the thread, not full thread OCR. Add P1 audit script before treating as production content. Tensor vs pipeline nuance is simplified; boss tier should add all-reduce overhead numeric intuition.
